question: Study the diagram of cross-section of an animal body showing different types of body cavities. Identify the pseudocoelomate condition.
diagram_description: Cross-section diagrams of animal bodies showing: (A) solid mesoderm with no cavity (acoelomate), (B) body cavity between mesoderm and endoderm (pseudocoelom), (C) body cavity lined by mesoderm on both sides (true coelom), (D) haemocoel filled with blood

question: The diagram shows the life cycle of Obelia. Identify the free-swimming larval stage.
diagram_description: Life cycle diagram of Obelia showing polyp colony, medusa bud formation, free-swimming medusa, gamete release, fertilization, and planula larva stages with arrows indicating sequence

question: Refer to the diagram showing different types of symmetry in animals. Which symmetry is shown by adult echinoderms?
diagram_description: Diagrams showing: spherical symmetry (any cut gives equal halves), radial symmetry (cuts along central axis give equal halves), bilateral symmetry (only one plane gives equal halves), and asymmetry (no plane gives equal halves)

question: The study of the diagram of Ascaris shows pseudocoelom. This body cavity develops from:
diagram_description: Transverse section of Ascaris showing outer cuticle, hypodermis, muscle layer, pseudocoelom (fluid-filled space), and internal organs including intestine and reproductive organs, with labels indicating the spaces between tissue layers

question: The diagram shows the cross-section of a starfish arm. Identify the water vascular system component.
diagram_description: Cross-section of starfish arm showing: epidermis, dermis, ambulacral groove with tube feet (podia), radial water canal running centrally, pyloric caecum, gonads, and coelom. Tube feet labeled with ampullae at top and sucker at bottom

question: The diagram shows different types of coelom. Which condition is shown by earthworm?
diagram_description: Four diagrams of animal cross-sections: (A) No body cavity between gut and body wall; (B) body cavity not lined by mesoderm (pseudocoelom); (C) body cavity fully lined by coelomic epithelium derived from mesoderm; (D) haemocoel with open circulatory system

question: Study the diagram of a generalized Arthropod. Identify the structure that allows moulting.
diagram_description: Diagram of arthropod body showing: chitinous exoskeleton, flexible intersegmental membranes between sclerites, moulting fluid space, epidermis (hypodermis) beneath old cuticle, ecdysone-secreting prothoracic gland location indicated, and new cuticle forming beneath old one

options.A: Flexible intersegmental membrane allows growth, old exoskeleton shed by ecdysis
options.D: Radula helps in breaking the shell

question: The diagram shows the canal system of a sponge. Identify the sycon type.
diagram_description: Three diagrams of sponge canal systems: (A) Ascon type – straight canals from pores to spongocoel; (B) Sycon type – radial canals branching from incurrent canals, choanocytes lining radial canals; (C) Leucon type – complex folded canals with flagellated chambers; arrows show water flow direction in each

question: The diagram shows the anatomy of Cockroach. How many pairs of legs does it have and to which class does it belong?
diagram_description: Dorsal view diagram of cockroach showing: head with antennae and compound eyes, thorax with pronotum, two pairs of wings (tegmina and hind wings), three pairs of jointed legs (one pair each on prothorax, mesothorax, metathorax), segmented abdomen with cerci

question: The diagram shows the life cycle of the silkworm (Bombyx mori). At which stage is silk produced?
diagram_description: Life cycle diagram of Bombyx mori (silkworm): Egg (ovum, laid on mulberry leaves) → Larva (silkworm/caterpillar, 4 moults over ~30 days, feeds on mulberry leaves) → Pupa (larva spins silk cocoon from single continuous silk thread 300–900m long, metamorphoses inside) → Adult moth (emerges from cocoon, mates, lays eggs). Arrow shows silk produced during larva-to-pupa transition. Silk glands (salivary glands modified) shown in larva

question: The diagram shows a fish pond (aquaculture system). Which type of culture rears different fish species at different water levels?
diagram_description: Diagram of composite fish culture pond showing: Surface zone – Catla (surface feeder, eats phytoplankton), Middle zone – Rohu (column feeder, eats zooplankton), Bottom zone – Mrigal/Common carp (bottom feeder, eats decaying organic matter), Grass carp (feeds on aquatic weeds along sides). This maximizes food utilization at all water levels. Typical stocking ratio shown. Fish illustrations at appropriate depths

options.B: Composite/Polyculture fish farming (multiple species at different levels)

question: The diagram shows the structure of a beehive (apiary). What is the role of the queen excluder?
diagram_description: Diagram of Langstroth beehive showing: Bottom board, Brood box (lower box with frames for eggs/larvae/pupae), Queen excluder (metal grid with holes too small for queen to pass but workers can), Honey super (upper boxes with frames for honey storage), Inner cover, Outer cover. Labels on each part. Worker bee and queen bee sizes compared showing why excluder works

options.B: To confine queen to brood box preventing her from laying eggs in honey supers

question: The diagram shows composite fish culture species. Which fish is a surface feeder?
diagram_description: Diagram of composite fish culture pond cross-section showing: Catla catla (large silver fish) near surface (surface feeder – eats phytoplankton and zooplankton at surface), Labeo rohita/Rohu (middle-level feeder – eats column zooplankton and decaying vegetation), Cirrhinus mrigala/Mrigal (bottom feeder – eats bottom mud and organic matter), Ctenopharyngodon idellus/Grass carp (edge feeder – eats aquatic weeds), Cyprinus carpio/Common carp (bottom omnivore)

question: The diagram shows the lac life cycle. When is seed lac (raw lac) harvested?
diagram_description: Life cycle of Kerria lacca on host tree: Female lac insects settle on young twigs, insert proboscis, secrete resinous lac encrustation (3–4mm thick crust around insect). Eggs laid inside lac shell. Nymphs (crawlers) emerge and settle on new twigs. Two crops per year: Baisakhi (summer crop harvested June) and Aghni/Katki (winter crop harvested October–November). Harvesting = cutting encrusted branches, scraping lac (stick lac) → seed lac → shellac processing

options.A: When insects are in egg stage
options.B: When mature female lac insects are encrusted on branches (twice yearly harvest)

question: The diagram shows the life cycle of the honey bee. How many days does it take for a worker bee to develop from egg to adult?
diagram_description: Table/diagram comparing development of Queen, Worker, and Drone bees: Egg stage: 3 days (all castes). Larval stage: Queen 5.5 days (fed royal jelly throughout), Worker 6 days (royal jelly for 2 days then bee bread), Drone 6.5 days. Pupal stage: Queen 7.5 days, Worker 12 days, Drone 14.5 days. Total: Queen ~16 days, Worker ~21 days, Drone ~24 days. Adult lifespan: Queen 2–5 years, Worker summer 6 weeks, Drone a few weeks

question: The diagram shows different types of poultry housing. Which system is considered most welfare-friendly?
diagram_description: Diagram comparing poultry housing systems: Battery cage system (small wire cages, high density, restricted movement – conventional eggs), Barn system (hens on floor in shed, larger space), Free-range system (access to outdoor areas during day, indoor at night), Organic/Free range (certified organic feed, outdoor access, lower density). Hen densities and space allowances labeled for each system. Egg type labels

options.C: Battery cage is best for welfare

question: The diagram shows the four levels of protein structure. What type of bonds maintain secondary structure?
diagram_description: Diagram showing four levels: (1) Primary – linear amino acid chain connected by peptide bonds; (2) Secondary – alpha helix (spiral, maintained by H-bonds between backbone NH and CO groups 4 residues apart) and beta sheet (parallel/antiparallel chains, H-bonds between strands); (3) Tertiary – 3D folding of entire polypeptide (H-bonds, ionic bonds, disulfide bridges, hydrophobic interactions); (4) Quaternary – multiple polypeptide subunits (haemoglobin – 2 alpha, 2 beta chains)

question: The diagram shows denaturation of a protein. What happens to primary structure during denaturation?
diagram_description: Diagram showing native protein (properly folded, active conformation) and denatured protein (unfolded, inactive). Denaturation causes: unfolding of tertiary and secondary structure (H-bonds, hydrophobic interactions broken), loss of biological activity. Primary structure (peptide bonds) INTACT. Caused by: heat, pH extremes, detergents, urea. Renaturation possible in some proteins (e.g., ribonuclease A). Denatured protein shown as random coil

options.A: Primary structure is destroyed (peptide bonds broken)
options.B: Primary structure (amino acid sequence) remains intact; only 3D folding is disrupted
options.C: All bonds including peptide bonds are broken

question: The diagram shows the structure of an alpha helix. How many amino acids are there per turn?
diagram_description: Diagram of alpha helix showing: right-handed helix, amino acid residues shown as spheres, backbone spiral, hydrogen bonds shown as dotted lines between N-H of one residue and C=O of residue 4 positions ahead (i to i+4 H-bonds). 3.6 amino acids per turn, pitch = 0.54nm, rise per residue = 0.15nm. Side chains projecting outward. Numbering of residues shown

question: The diagram shows the structure of haemoglobin. What level of protein structure does haemoglobin demonstrate?
diagram_description: Diagram of haemoglobin molecule showing: 4 polypeptide chains (2 alpha chains + 2 beta chains in HbA), each chain separately folded (tertiary structure), each associated with one haem group. Four chains assembled together into one functional protein (quaternary structure). Arrows showing: primary (sequence), secondary (helical segments in each chain), tertiary (3D of each chain), quaternary (4-chain assembly)

options.B: Quaternary structure (4 polypeptide subunits assembled together)

question: The diagram shows water-soluble vs fat-soluble vitamins. Which water-soluble vitamin acts as antioxidant?
diagram_description: Table/diagram showing vitamins classified by solubility: Fat-soluble (stored in liver/adipose – A, D, E, K). Water-soluble (not stored, excreted in urine – B complex: B1 thiamine, B2 riboflavin, B3 niacin, B5 pantothenic acid, B6 pyridoxine, B7 biotin, B9 folate, B12 cobalamin; and Vitamin C ascorbic acid). Functions listed for each. Vitamin C and Vitamin E labeled as antioxidants. Water-soluble vitamins highlighted

question: The diagram shows enzyme-substrate interaction. What makes an enzyme specific for its substrate?
diagram_description: Diagram showing enzyme active site complementarity: enzyme with specific 3D active site cleft. Correct substrate fits perfectly (induced fit model – enzyme shape adjusts slightly on binding). Enzyme-substrate complex formed. Products released, enzyme unchanged. Wrong substrate shown not fitting. Lock-and-key model (rigid) vs induced fit model (flexible) compared. Active site amino acids make specific interactions

options.B: Complementary 3D shape of active site to substrate (induced fit)

question: The diagram shows amino acid structure. Which part varies between different amino acids?
diagram_description: General amino acid structure diagram showing: central alpha carbon (Cα), amino group (-NH2) on left, carboxyl group (-COOH) on right, hydrogen atom below, and R group (side chain) above. R group labeled as variable part. Example R groups shown: glycine (H), alanine (CH3), serine (CH2OH), cysteine (CH2SH), phenylalanine (benzyl group). Zwitterion form at physiological pH also shown

question: The diagram shows biotin (Vitamin B7) function. Biotin acts as coenzyme for:
diagram_description: Diagram showing biotin structure (bicyclic ring system) and its role as cofactor: Biotin attached to enzyme via lysine residue (biocytin). Carboxylation reactions: Pyruvate + CO2 → Oxaloacetate (pyruvate carboxylase, biotin). Acetyl-CoA + CO2 → Malonyl-CoA (acetyl-CoA carboxylase for fatty acid synthesis, biotin). Biotin acts as CO2 carrier. Deficiency caused by raw egg white consumption (avidin binds biotin)

question: The diagram shows gel electrophoresis. How do DNA fragments separate?
diagram_description: Diagram of agarose gel electrophoresis: Negative electrode (cathode) at top, positive electrode (anode) at bottom. DNA loaded in wells at top (near negative). DNA is negatively charged (phosphate groups) → migrates toward positive electrode. Smaller fragments move faster through agarose pores, travel further down gel. Larger fragments move slower, remain near top. Bands visualized by ethidium bromide staining under UV light. DNA ladder (size markers) in one lane

options.B: Smaller fragments move further toward positive electrode

question: The diagram shows the steps of PCR. What is the purpose of the denaturation step?
diagram_description: PCR cycle diagram showing three steps: (1) Denaturation at 94–95°C – hydrogen bonds between DNA strands break, two single strands formed; (2) Annealing at 50–65°C – primers bind to complementary sequences on each strand; (3) Extension at 72°C – Taq polymerase synthesizes new strand from primer using dNTPs. Each cycle doubles DNA amount. After 30 cycles: 2^30 = ~1 billion copies. Thermocycler machine shown

options.B: Separate double-stranded DNA into single strands

question: The diagram shows construction of recombinant DNA. Which enzyme joins the insert and vector?
diagram_description: Diagram of recombinant DNA construction: Plasmid cut with restriction enzyme (EcoRI) → linear plasmid with sticky ends. Gene of interest from donor DNA cut with same EcoRI → gene with complementary sticky ends. Sticky end annealing brings gene into plasmid. DNA ligase joins the phosphodiester bonds sealing the nicks. Recombinant plasmid formed. Transformed into E. coli. Colony selection on antibiotic plates

question: The diagram shows DNA fingerprinting comparing crime scene sample with suspects. How is the DNA profile used as evidence?
diagram_description: Gel electrophoresis diagram of DNA fingerprinting: Lanes labeled Crime Scene, Suspect 1, Suspect 2, Suspect 3, Victim. VNTR bands visualized. Crime scene profile matches Suspect 2 exactly (same band pattern). Suspect 1 and 3 show different patterns. The probability of two unrelated individuals having the same VNTR profile is extremely low (~1 in billions). STR (Short Tandem Repeat) analysis shown as modern method

options.B: Matching VNTR/STR band pattern between crime scene sample and suspect identifies individual

question: The diagram shows ELISA steps. In sandwich ELISA, what does the colour change indicate?
diagram_description: Sandwich ELISA diagram: (1) Capture antibody coated on well; (2) Sample added – antigen binds if present; (3) Wash; (4) Detection antibody (enzyme-linked) added – binds antigen; (5) Wash; (6) Substrate added → enzyme converts substrate to coloured product; (7) Colour measured by spectrophotometer (absorbance). Positive well: coloured. Negative well: colourless. Quantification possible by standard curve

question: The diagram shows the steps of DNA fingerprinting. Which step uses a radioactive or fluorescent probe?
diagram_description: DNA fingerprinting steps: (1) DNA extraction from sample; (2) Restriction enzyme digestion of DNA at VNTR sites; (3) Gel electrophoresis – fragments separated by size; (4) Southern blot – DNA transferred from gel to nitrocellulose/nylon membrane; (5) Hybridization – radioactive/fluorescent VNTR probes added, bind complementary sequences; (6) Autoradiography – X-ray film/imaging shows bands where probe bound; (7) Band pattern compared between samples

question: The diagram shows insulin gene cloning. Why is cDNA used instead of genomic DNA for expressing insulin in bacteria?
diagram_description: Diagram comparing: (A) Genomic insulin gene – contains introns (non-coding, processed out in eukaryotes) which bacteria CANNOT remove → non-functional protein. (B) cDNA approach: isolate insulin mRNA from pancreatic beta cells → reverse transcriptase makes cDNA → cDNA has no introns → insert into expression vector → bacteria can transcribe and translate correctly → functional human insulin produced

options.B: Bacteria cannot process introns, so intron-free cDNA is needed for functional expression
options.C: cDNA is easier to cut with restriction enzymes

question: The diagram shows the cardiac cycle. What is the duration of one cardiac cycle at 75 beats/minute?
diagram_description: Graph of cardiac cycle showing pressure changes over time: atrial systole (0.1s), ventricular systole (0.3s), joint diastole (0.4s). Pressure curves for left atrium, left ventricle, and aorta shown with valve opening/closing events marked. Total cycle = 0.8 seconds

question: The diagram shows the structure of the human heart. Identify the valve between the left atrium and left ventricle.
diagram_description: Frontal section of human heart showing: right atrium (RA), right ventricle (RV), left atrium (LA), left ventricle (LV), superior and inferior vena cava entering RA, pulmonary artery leaving RV, pulmonary veins entering LA, aorta leaving LV, tricuspid valve (between RA and RV), bicuspid/mitral valve (between LA and LV), pulmonary semilunar valve, aortic semilunar valve, chordae tendineae, papillary muscles

question: The diagram shows the blood clotting cascade. Which factor is the final common pathway enzyme that converts fibrinogen to fibrin?
diagram_description: Simplified clotting cascade: Intrinsic pathway (contact activation) and Extrinsic pathway (tissue factor) converging at Factor X activation. Factor Xa + Factor Va (prothrombinase complex) converts Prothrombin (Factor II) → Thrombin (Factor IIa). Thrombin converts Fibrinogen (Factor I) → Fibrin monomers → Factor XIIIa cross-links to stable fibrin clot

question: The diagram shows an ECG tracing. Which wave represents ventricular depolarization?
diagram_description: ECG tracing showing: P wave (small, atrial depolarization), PR interval (conduction through AV node), QRS complex (ventricular depolarization – tall narrow deflections), ST segment (early ventricular repolarization), T wave (ventricular repolarization), with time axis (each small square = 0.04s) and voltage axis labeled

question: The diagram shows capillary exchange. What force drives fluid OUT of capillaries at the arterial end?
diagram_description: Diagram of capillary showing: arteriole end (hydrostatic pressure=35mmHg, oncotic pressure=25mmHg, net filtration=10mmHg), mid-capillary, venule end (hydrostatic pressure=15mmHg, oncotic pressure=25mmHg, net absorption=10mmHg). Protein (albumin) remaining in capillary creating oncotic pressure. Tissue fluid space around capillary. Lymph capillary collecting excess fluid

question: The diagram shows blood groups. Which blood group has both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in plasma?
diagram_description: Table/diagram showing ABO blood groups: Group A (antigen A on RBC, anti-B antibody in plasma), Group B (antigen B on RBC, anti-A antibody in plasma), Group AB (antigens A and B on RBC, no antibodies in plasma – universal recipient), Group O (no antigens on RBC, both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in plasma – universal donor)

question: The diagram shows the structure of an artery, vein, and capillary. Which vessel has the thickest tunica media?
diagram_description: Cross-section diagrams of three vessels: (A) Artery – thick tunica intima, very thick tunica media with smooth muscle and elastic fibres, tunica adventitia; (B) Vein – thin tunica media, valves present, large lumen; (C) Capillary – single layer of endothelium only, basement membrane, pericytes

question: The diagram shows a normal ECG. What is the PR interval significance?
diagram_description: ECG tracing with labeled intervals: P wave onset to QRS onset = PR interval (0.12-0.20s, represents time for impulse to travel from SA node through AV node to ventricular muscle). QRS duration = 0.06-0.10s. QT interval = time for ventricular depolarization and repolarization. Each annotation with normal values shown

options.C: Conduction time from SA node to ventricles through AV node

question: The diagram shows lymphatic capillaries. What is the unique feature of lymph capillaries compared to blood capillaries?
diagram_description: Comparison diagram: Blood capillary (closed tube, tight junctions between endothelial cells, continuous basement membrane, two-way exchange). Lymph capillary (blind-ended, overlapping endothelial cells acting as one-way valves, discontinuous basement membrane, only absorbs fluid from tissues, larger lumen, valves present in larger lymphatics)

options.B: They are blind-ended with one-way flap valves allowing easy entry of tissue fluid
options.D: They are present in all tissues including cartilage

question: The diagram shows the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve. What happens to the curve when CO2 increases (Bohr effect)?
diagram_description: Graph showing O2-Hb dissociation curve (% saturation of Hb vs pO2). Normal sigmoid curve shown. Second curve shifted to the right labeled 'increased CO2/decreased pH (Bohr effect)'. Third curve shifted to left labeled 'decreased CO2/increased pH'. Points at pO2=40 and pO2=100 mmHg marked

options.B: Curve shifts to right (lower affinity, more O2 released)

question: The diagram shows the structure of the human respiratory system. Identify the structure that prevents food from entering the trachea.
diagram_description: Diagram of upper respiratory tract showing: nasal cavity, pharynx, larynx with epiglottis (leaf-shaped flap at top of larynx), glottis, trachea with C-shaped cartilage rings, bifurcation into left and right bronchi, lung lobes. Epiglottis position shown during swallowing (covering glottis) and breathing (upright, glottis open)

question: The diagram shows gas exchange at the alveolar surface. Which gas moves from alveolus to blood?
diagram_description: Diagram of alveolus and adjacent pulmonary capillary showing: alveolar air (pO2=104mmHg, pCO2=40mmHg), deoxygenated blood entering (pO2=40mmHg, pCO2=45mmHg), gas exchange across thin alveolar-capillary membrane (two cell layers + basement membrane = 0.5 micron), oxygenated blood leaving (pO2=95mmHg, pCO2=40mmHg). Arrows showing O2 and CO2 movements

options.A: O2 moves from blood to alveolus
options.B: CO2 moves from alveolus to blood
options.C: O2 moves from alveolus to blood
options.D: Both gases move from alveolus to blood

question: The diagram shows lung volumes and capacities. What is the total lung capacity (TLC) approximately?
diagram_description: Spirometer tracing showing lung volumes: RV=1200mL, ERV=1100mL, TV=500mL, IRV=3000mL. Capacities derived: FRC=RV+ERV=2300mL, VC=IRV+TV+ERV=4600mL, TLC=VC+RV=5800mL. Graph shows normal breathing then maximal inspiration and expiration with all volumes labeled

question: The diagram shows the structure of haemoglobin. How many O2 molecules can one Hb molecule carry?
diagram_description: Diagram of haemoglobin molecule showing: 4 globin chains (2 alpha, 2 beta chains in HbA), each chain associated with one haem group (iron-containing porphyrin ring), Fe2+ ion at center of each haem group binding one O2 molecule, quaternary structure showing all 4 subunits together

question: The diagram shows the mechanism of CO2 transport. In which form is most CO2 transported?
diagram_description: Diagram showing CO2 produced in tissue cells entering RBC. Three pathways: (1) ~7% dissolved in plasma; (2) ~23% as carbaminoHb binding to globin chains; (3) ~70% reacting with H2O via carbonic anhydrase to form H2CO3, which dissociates to H+ (buffered by Hb) and HCO3- (exits RBC via chloride shift). Percentages labeled on each pathway

question: The diagram shows a cross-section of the trachea. What prevents the trachea from collapsing?
diagram_description: Cross-section of trachea showing: pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium lining, C-shaped cartilage rings (incomplete posteriorly), trachealis muscle (smooth muscle) completing the ring posteriorly, submucosa with goblet cells and seromucous glands, adventitia on outside. Labeled: cartilage C-rings, trachealis muscle, lumen, epithelium

question: The diagram compares normal alveoli with emphysematous alveoli. What is the main change in emphysema?
diagram_description: Side-by-side comparison: Normal lung showing small numerous alveoli with thin walls, extensive capillary network, large total surface area (70 m²). Emphysematous lung showing enlarged air spaces due to destruction of alveolar walls (breakdown of elastin), fewer but larger alveoli, reduced capillary bed, reduced total surface area, loss of elastic recoil

options.B: Loss of alveolar walls reducing surface area

question: The diagram shows gas partial pressures at different points in the circulation. What is the pO2 of venous blood returning to lungs?
diagram_description: Diagram of circulation showing pO2 and pCO2 values: atmospheric air (pO2=159, pCO2=0.3 mmHg), alveolar air (pO2=104, pCO2=40), arterial blood leaving lungs (pO2=95, pCO2=40), tissue capillaries where O2 is consumed and CO2 produced, venous blood returning to lungs (pO2=40, pCO2=45 mmHg)

question: The diagram shows the hypothalamo-pituitary axis. Which type of feedback inhibits TRH/TSH when T3/T4 are high?
diagram_description: Diagram of HPT axis: Hypothalamus secretes TRH (Thyrotropin-releasing hormone) → Anterior pituitary secretes TSH (Thyroid-stimulating hormone) → Thyroid gland secretes T3 and T4. Negative feedback arrows shown: T3/T4 inhibiting both hypothalamus (long-loop) and anterior pituitary (short-loop). When T3/T4 high → less TRH and TSH → thyroid produces less hormone

question: The diagram shows the structure of the adrenal gland. Which zone of the cortex produces cortisol?
diagram_description: Cross-section of adrenal gland showing outer cortex with three zones (from outside in): Zona glomerulosa (produces mineralocorticoids – aldosterone), Zona fasciculata (large, lipid-filled cells producing glucocorticoids – cortisol, the thickest zone), Zona reticularis (produces androgens – DHEA), and inner Adrenal medulla (chromaffin cells producing catecholamines – epinephrine/norepinephrine)

question: The diagram shows hormone action via intracellular receptors. Which type of hormone uses this mechanism?
diagram_description: Two mechanisms shown: (A) Peptide/protein hormone – binds cell surface receptor → second messenger (cAMP via adenylyl cyclase) → protein kinase cascade → cellular response; (B) Steroid/thyroid hormone – lipid soluble, crosses plasma membrane, binds intracellular receptor in cytoplasm or nucleus, hormone-receptor complex binds DNA at HRE → gene transcription → protein synthesis → cellular response

question: The diagram shows blood glucose regulation. In diabetes mellitus Type 2, what is the primary defect?
diagram_description: Graph showing blood glucose after meal: normal curve (rises then falls back to 80-100mg/dL by 2hr), Type 1 DM (very high, no insulin secretion, no decrease), Type 2 DM (high, stays elevated longer – insulin produced but cells resistant). Feedback loop: high glucose → beta cells → insulin → cells take up glucose → glucose decreases → less insulin. In T2DM: receptor defect on target cells

options.B: Insulin resistance in target cells (receptors dysfunctional)

question: The diagram shows the negative feedback in cortisol regulation. What happens when cortisol levels rise?
diagram_description: Flow diagram: Stress → Hypothalamus releases CRH → Anterior pituitary releases ACTH → Adrenal cortex releases Cortisol. Negative feedback: High cortisol inhibits both hypothalamus (reduces CRH) and anterior pituitary (reduces ACTH sensitivity). Net result: cortisol falls back to normal level. Second loop shown for primary adrenal insufficiency (low cortisol → high CRH, ACTH)

question: The diagram shows the location of endocrine glands. Which gland is located at the base of the brain attached to hypothalamus?
diagram_description: Human body diagram with endocrine glands labeled: Brain with pineal gland (dorsal) and pituitary gland (ventral, attached to hypothalamus by infundibulum stalk), thyroid and parathyroids (neck), thymus (chest), adrenal glands (on top of kidneys), pancreas (abdomen, behind stomach), ovaries (female pelvis) or testes (male)

question: The diagram shows the effects of insulin vs glucagon on blood glucose homeostasis. Which hormone is secreted when blood glucose falls below 80 mg/dL?
diagram_description: Blood glucose homeostasis diagram: Blood glucose rises after meal → beta cells secrete insulin → glucose uptake by liver (glycogenesis), muscle, adipose → blood glucose falls to normal (80–100mg/dL). Blood glucose falls (fasting/exercise) → alpha cells secrete glucagon → liver glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis → glucose released → blood glucose rises to normal. Set point shown

question: The diagram shows the menstrual cycle hormone levels. Which hormone surge triggers ovulation?
diagram_description: Graph of female reproductive cycle showing: FSH levels (rises early follicular phase, slight rise at ovulation), LH levels (major mid-cycle LH surge on day 14 triggering ovulation), Estrogen (gradual rise in follicular phase, peak just before ovulation, second rise in luteal phase), Progesterone (low in follicular phase, high in luteal phase after ovulation). Ovulation marked on day 14

question: The diagram shows the structure of a villus in the small intestine. Which vessel is responsible for absorbing fatty acids and glycerol?
diagram_description: Cross-section diagram of small intestinal villus showing: columnar epithelium with microvilli (brush border) at top, capillary network (blood vessels) in core, central lacteal (lymph capillary), goblet cells between columnar cells, lamina propria, lymphocyte infiltration

question: The diagram shows different parts of the human digestive system. What is the pH at which pepsin works most effectively?
diagram_description: Diagram of human digestive system showing esophagus, stomach, small intestine (duodenum, jejunum, ileum), large intestine, with a pH scale alongside each segment: mouth (6.5–7.5), stomach (1.5–3.5), duodenum (6–7), jejunum and ileum (7–8), colon (5.5–7)

question: The diagram shows the process of fat digestion. What are the small droplets formed during emulsification called?
diagram_description: Diagram showing fat digestion process: large fat globule (triglyceride), bile salts acting as emulsifying agents surrounding fat, formation of small micelles/emulsion droplets, pancreatic lipase acting on surface, release of fatty acids and monoglycerides, reassembly into chylomicrons in enterocyte, exocytosis into lacteal

question: The diagram shows the structure of the stomach wall. Identify the cells that secrete HCl.
diagram_description: Cross-section of gastric mucosa showing gastric pit opening to surface, mucous neck cells near top of glands, parietal (oxyntic) cells (large, pale, in middle region of gland secreting HCl), chief (zymogenic) cells (smaller, darker, at base secreting pepsinogen), enteroendocrine cells at base

question: The diagram shows absorption mechanisms across the intestinal epithelium. Which molecule uses co-transport with Na+ for absorption?
diagram_description: Diagram of intestinal epithelial cell showing apical membrane (brush border) facing intestinal lumen and basolateral membrane facing blood. Na+/Glucose co-transporter (SGLT1) on apical membrane, GLUT2 on basolateral membrane, Na+/K+ ATPase on basolateral membrane, amino acid transporters, fatty acid diffusion, fructose via GLUT5

question: The diagram shows the liver lobule. Which vessel receives oxygenated blood?
diagram_description: Diagram of liver lobule showing: central vein at center, hepatocytes arranged in radiating cords, sinusoids (liver capillaries) between cords, portal triad at corners consisting of hepatic artery (small), portal vein (large), and bile duct; Kupffer cells lining sinusoids; bile canaliculi between hepatocytes draining to bile duct

question: The diagram shows the brush border enzymes. Which enzyme splits lactose?
diagram_description: Diagram of intestinal brush border membrane showing microvilli with embedded enzymes: maltase (splits maltose → glucose+glucose), sucrase (splits sucrose → glucose+fructose), lactase (splits lactose → glucose+galactose), aminopeptidase (cleaves amino acids from peptides), dipeptidase (splits dipeptides)

question: The diagram shows the process of protein digestion. What are the final products entering the blood?
diagram_description: Flow diagram of protein digestion: Protein → Pepsin (stomach) → Polypeptides → Trypsin/Chymotrypsin (duodenum) → Smaller peptides → Carboxypeptidase/Aminopeptidase (brush border) → Dipeptides/Amino acids → Dipeptidase → Free amino acids → absorbed into capillaries via active transport

question: The diagram shows the geological time scale. In which era did dinosaurs flourish?
diagram_description: Geological time scale diagram showing eons, eras, periods: Precambrian (4600–541 Mya, origin of life, prokaryotes, eukaryotes), Paleozoic (541–252 Mya, fish, amphibians, reptiles appear), Mesozoic (252–66 Mya – Triassic/Jurassic/Cretaceous, dinosaurs dominate), Cenozoic (66 Mya–present, mammals diversify, hominids, human evolution). Each era labeled with major events

question: The diagram shows Darwin's finches with different beak shapes. What type of evolution does this demonstrate?
diagram_description: Diagram of Galapagos finches showing multiple species with different beak shapes: Large ground finch (thick beak for crushing seeds), Cactus finch (curved beak for cactus), Warbler finch (thin beak for insects), Woodpecker finch (uses tools). All descended from common finch ancestor. Map of Galapagos Islands showing isolation of populations on different islands

options.B: Adaptive radiation (divergent evolution from one ancestor)

question: The diagram shows comparative embryology across vertebrates. What conclusion does this evidence support?
diagram_description: Diagram showing early embryo stages of fish, amphibian, reptile, bird, and human side by side at three developmental stages. Early stage: all show pharyngeal gill arches, similar curved body shape, tail present. Middle stage: divergence begins. Late stage: species-specific features emerge. Labels on each embryo type

options.B: Early embryos of different vertebrates are remarkably similar, showing common ancestry
options.C: Embryos develop at the same rate

question: The diagram shows three types of natural selection curves. Which type increases frequency of extreme phenotypes?
diagram_description: Three graphs of population distribution before and after selection: (A) Stabilizing – bell curve narrows, extremes eliminated, mean preserved; (B) Directional – bell curve shifts to one side, one extreme favored; (C) Disruptive/Diversifying – bimodal distribution formed, both extremes favored, intermediate eliminated. Arrows showing selection pressure in each case

question: The diagram shows Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. If q² = 0.09 (frequency of affected recessive), what is carrier frequency (2pq)?
diagram_description: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium diagram: p + q = 1. Genotype frequencies: p²(AA) + 2pq(Aa) + q²(aa) = 1. Example: q² = 0.09, so q = 0.3, p = 0.7. Carrier frequency 2pq = 2 × 0.7 × 0.3 = 0.42 (42%). Pie chart showing proportion of each genotype in population. Graph of frequencies at equilibrium

question: The diagram shows homologous forelimb structures of different vertebrates. What evolutionary principle does this support?
diagram_description: Diagram comparing forelimb bones of: Human (arm bones – humerus, radius, ulna, carpals, metacarpals, phalanges for manipulation), Bat (same bones elongated for flight membrane), Whale (same bones compressed and paddle-shaped for swimming), Horse (same bones with fused and reduced digits for running). All labeled with same bone names showing homology

options.B: Divergent evolution from a common ancestor (homologous structures)

question: The diagram shows human evolution timeline. What is the correct sequence?
diagram_description: Timeline of human evolution showing hominid species and approximate dates: Sahelanthropus tchadensis (7 Mya), Ardipithecus (4.4 Mya), Australopithecus afarensis/Lucy (3.2 Mya), Homo habilis (2.5 Mya, first tools), Homo erectus (1.9 Mya, fire, migrated to Asia), Homo heidelbergensis (0.7 Mya), Homo neanderthalensis (0.4–0.04 Mya, Europe), Homo sapiens (0.3 Mya, Africa). Silhouettes showing increasing upright posture and brain size

options.A: Homo erectus → Australopithecus → Homo habilis → Homo sapiens
options.B: Australopithecus → Homo habilis → Homo erectus → Homo sapiens

question: The diagram shows antibiotic resistance evolution in bacteria. Which mechanism best explains this?
diagram_description: Diagram showing antibiotic resistance evolution: Large bacterial population with mostly sensitive bacteria (red) and rare resistant mutants (blue). Antibiotic applied – sensitive bacteria killed, resistant survive and reproduce. After several generations – population now mostly resistant. This demonstrates: pre-existing variation + selection pressure = evolution. NOT Lamarckian (bacteria did not acquire resistance in response to antibiotic)

options.A: Bacteria mutated in response to antibiotic (Lamarckian)
options.B: Pre-existing resistant mutants were selected by antibiotic (Darwinian natural selection)
options.D: Antibiotic caused DNA repair in bacteria

question: The diagram shows the structure of a nephron. Which part performs maximum reabsorption of glucose and amino acids?
diagram_description: Diagram of nephron showing: Bowman's capsule with glomerulus (filtration), Proximal Convoluted Tubule PCT (major reabsorption), Loop of Henle descending and ascending limbs (concentration), Distal Convoluted Tubule DCT (fine-tuning), Collecting Duct (water reabsorption under ADH). Peritubular capillaries surrounding tubules. Arrows showing reabsorption and secretion

question: The diagram shows the process of urine formation. Which process involves movement of substances from peritubular capillaries into the tubular lumen?
diagram_description: Nephron diagram with arrows showing three processes: (1) Filtration – blood pressure forces fluid from glomerular capillaries into Bowman's capsule; (2) Reabsorption – substances move from tubule lumen back into peritubular capillaries; (3) Secretion – substances move from peritubular capillaries INTO tubule lumen (opposite direction to reabsorption). Each process labeled with examples

question: The diagram shows the RAAS (Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System). What does Angiotensin II do?
diagram_description: Flow diagram of RAAS: Low blood pressure/volume → JGA releases Renin → Renin converts Angiotensinogen (liver) → Angiotensin I → ACE (lungs) converts → Angiotensin II → multiple effects: vasoconstriction (increases BP), stimulates adrenal cortex to release Aldosterone (Na+ reabsorption → water retention), stimulates ADH release, stimulates thirst center

options.B: Vasoconstriction and stimulation of aldosterone release

question: The diagram shows a cross-section of the kidney. Which region has the highest osmolarity?
diagram_description: Cross-section of kidney showing: cortex (outer region, osmolarity ~300 mOsm), outer medulla (300–600 mOsm gradient), inner medulla (600–1200 mOsm at papilla tip), renal pelvis, ureter. Nephrons shown with glomeruli in cortex, loops of Henle descending into medulla creating gradient, collecting ducts passing through medulla to pelvis

question: The diagram shows the juxtaglomerular apparatus. Which cells secrete renin?
diagram_description: Diagram of JGA showing afferent arteriole approaching glomerulus, juxtaglomerular (granular) cells in the wall of afferent arteriole (contain renin granules), macula densa cells (modified DCT cells sensing NaCl concentration), extraglomerular mesangial cells between the two, efferent arteriole leaving glomerulus. Labels on each component

question: The diagram shows the countercurrent exchange in vasa recta. What is the purpose of this mechanism?
diagram_description: Diagram showing vasa recta (hairpin-shaped blood vessels) descending alongside descending limb of Loop of Henle. As blood descends: NaCl enters blood, water leaves (osmolarity increases). As blood ascends: NaCl leaves blood, water enters (osmolarity decreases). Net effect: medullary interstitium osmolarity gradient maintained despite blood flow through medulla

options.A: To deliver O2 to tubule cells only
options.B: To maintain medullary osmolarity gradient without washing it away
options.C: To remove waste directly from tubules
options.D: To concentrate urine in collecting duct

question: The diagram shows the filtration membrane of glomerulus. Which layer prevents large proteins from entering filtrate?
diagram_description: Diagram of glomerular filtration membrane showing three layers: (1) Fenestrated capillary endothelium (large fenestrae, allows most substances through except cells); (2) Basement membrane (fused, negatively charged, size and charge selective barrier blocking albumin); (3) Podocytes with foot processes (pedicels) and filtration slits. Red blood cell shown too large to pass. Albumin shown blocked by basement membrane

question: The diagram shows changes in osmolarity along the nephron. Where does the filtrate become most hyperosmotic compared to plasma?
diagram_description: Graph of tubular fluid osmolarity vs nephron segments: Bowman's capsule/PCT (300 mOsm, isosmotic), descending Loop of Henle (increases to 1200 mOsm at bottom of loop), ascending Loop of Henle (decreases back to ~100 mOsm – hypoosmotic at macula densa), DCT (variable), collecting duct (increases back to 1200 mOsm in medulla under ADH)

options.B: Top of ascending Loop of Henle
options.C: Bottom of descending Loop of Henle

question: The diagram shows a pedigree of X-linked recessive colour blindness. What pattern confirms X-linked recessive inheritance?
diagram_description: Pedigree chart with squares=males, circles=females, shaded=affected, half-shaded=carrier. Generation I: normal parents. Generation II: carrier female, normal male, affected male. Generation III: children of carrier female x normal male show 25% affected males. Key features: no father-to-son transmission of trait, carrier mothers pass to sons, females rarely affected unless homozygous.

question: The diagram shows human karyotype of Down syndrome. Which chromosome shows trisomy?
diagram_description: Karyotype image with chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs. Chromosomes 1-22 shown in homologous pairs. Chromosome 21 pair shows THREE chromosomes (trisomy 21) instead of two. Total chromosome number = 47. Chromosomes arranged from largest (1) to smallest (21) with sex chromosomes XX or XY separate. Down syndrome features listed: intellectual disability, flat face, epicanthal folds, Brushfield spots.

question: The diagram shows sex determination. The sex of offspring is determined by:
diagram_description: Punnett square showing: Mother XX produces only X gametes. Father XY produces X and Y gametes (50% each). Cross: X+X = XX (female 50%), X+Y = XY (male 50%). SRY gene on Y chromosome initiates testis development. Diagram confirms sex determined by father's sperm (X or Y).

question: The diagram shows crossing over between homologous chromosomes. At what stage does crossing over occur?
diagram_description: Diagram of meiosis I prophase sub-stages: leptotene (chromosomes condense), zygotene (homologues pair/synapse), pachytene (bivalents fully formed, crossing over occurs between non-sister chromatids at chiasmata), diplotene (chiasmata visible), diakinesis (bivalents maximally condensed). Second diagram shows two homologous chromosomes with synaptonemal complex and chiasma points.

question: The diagram shows chromosomal structural mutations. Which type involves segment reversal?
diagram_description: Four diagrams of chromosomal structural changes with before/after: (A) Deletion - segment lost, chromosome shorter; (B) Duplication - extra copy of segment, chromosome longer; (C) Inversion - segment detached and reattached in reversed orientation (paracentric if doesn't include centromere, pericentric if includes centromere); (D) Translocation - segment moved to non-homologous chromosome. Each shown with chromosome diagrams.

question: The diagram shows a pedigree of autosomal dominant disorder with affected heterozygous parent (Aa) x normal (aa). What proportion of children are affected?
diagram_description: Pedigree showing affected father (Aa, shaded square) married to normal mother (aa, open circle). Punnett square: Aa x aa = 50% Aa (affected) + 50% aa (normal). Children shown: 2 affected, 2 normal. Features of autosomal dominant: every generation affected, father-to-son transmission possible, 50% offspring affected if one parent heterozygous.

question: The diagram shows the pedigree with haemophiliac son from unaffected parents. Which parent is the carrier?
diagram_description: Pedigree: Generation I: normal female x normal male. Generation II: three children: normal daughter, normal son, haemophiliac son (shaded square). Analysis: Father is XY unaffected so cannot carry X^h on Y. Son inherited X from mother and Y from father. Since son is X^h Y, he got X^h from mother. Therefore mother is carrier X^H X^h. Father only contributes Y to son.

question: The diagram shows the structure of an antibody. Which region is responsible for antigen binding?
diagram_description: Diagram of IgG antibody showing: Y-shaped structure with two heavy chains and two light chains linked by disulfide bonds. Variable regions (VH and VL) at tips of Y forming two identical antigen-binding sites (Fab regions). Constant region (Fc) at stem interacts with complement and effector cells. Hinge region for flexibility. CDR (complementarity determining regions) at variable tips labeled as antigen binding sites

question: The diagram shows the life cycle of Plasmodium. In which organ does the exo-erythrocytic cycle occur?
diagram_description: Life cycle of Plasmodium: Infected female Anopheles mosquito injects sporozoites into human blood → sporozoites travel to liver (exo-erythrocytic stage: liver cells invaded, merozoites produced) → merozoites enter RBCs (erythrocytic cycle: ring stage, trophozoite, schizont, merozoites released, some become gametocytes) → gametocytes taken up by mosquito (sexual cycle in mosquito gut: gametes, zygote, oocyst, sporozoites)

question: The diagram shows the immune response to a vaccine. What is the significance of the secondary response?
diagram_description: Graph showing antibody titre vs time: Primary response after first antigen exposure (slow rise over 2 weeks, lower peak, mostly IgM). Memory cells formed. Secondary response after second antigen exposure (same or booster vaccine) – faster (within days), higher antibody titre, longer lasting, mainly IgG. This is basis of booster doses in vaccination. Memory B and T cells shown persisting between exposures

options.A: Secondary response is weaker than primary
options.B: Secondary response is faster and stronger due to immunological memory cells
options.D: Secondary response requires a new vaccine

question: The diagram shows different types of T cells and their functions. Which T cell directly kills infected cells?
diagram_description: Diagram of T cell types: CD4+ T helper cells (recognise MHC II, secrete cytokines, help B cells and CTLs), CD8+ Cytotoxic T lymphocytes/CTL (recognise MHC I on infected cells, release perforin and granzymes killing target cell), T regulatory cells (suppress excessive immune response), Memory T cells (long-lived, rapid secondary response). Each cell type with function arrows shown

question: The diagram shows the antigen-antibody interaction and agglutination. What type of immunity is this?
diagram_description: Diagram showing: Antigen (bacterial surface) binding to specific antibody (IgG or IgM). Multiple antibody-antigen complexes forming crosslinks between bacteria → agglutination (clumping). Complement activation shown following antibody binding → membrane attack complex → lysis. Opsonization shown (antibody Fc region recognized by macrophage Fc receptors → phagocytosis enhanced). Labels on each step

question: The diagram shows malaria symptoms timeline. The 48–72 hour cycle of fever corresponds to which event?
diagram_description: Timeline diagram of malaria cycle: Sporozoite enters liver (10–14 day incubation, no symptoms). Merozoites enter RBCs. In RBC: ring stage → trophozoite → schizont → rupture releases merozoites + pyrogens every 48 hrs (P. vivax, P. falciparum – tertian malaria) or 72 hrs (P. malariae – quartan malaria). Graph showing fever spikes every 48 or 72 hours corresponding to synchronized RBC rupture

options.B: Rupture of infected RBCs releasing merozoites and pyrogens

question: The diagram shows cancer development stages. What is the term for transformation of normal cell into cancer cell?
diagram_description: Diagram of cancer progression: Normal cell → DNA damage by carcinogen/mutation → mutated cell (proto-oncogene becomes oncogene or tumour suppressor mutated) → malignant transformation (loss of contact inhibition, immortalization) → primary tumour (benign stage, contained) → invasion of basement membrane → metastasis (cancer cells in lymph or blood vessels, colonize distant organs). Each stage labeled with molecular changes

question: The diagram shows how vaccines work. Which cells are responsible for immunological memory?
diagram_description: Vaccine mechanism diagram: Vaccine antigen (attenuated pathogen/toxoid/subunit) injected → APC (dendritic cells) present antigen via MHC → Naive B and T cells activated → clonal expansion → effector cells (plasma cells secreting antibodies, cytotoxic T cells) + Memory B cells and Memory T cells (long-lived, dormant). Second exposure to pathogen → rapid activation of memory cells → faster, larger immune response

options.B: Memory B cells and Memory T cells

question: The diagram shows spermatogenesis. What is the ploidy of a primary spermatocyte?
diagram_description: Diagram of spermatogenesis in seminiferous tubule showing: spermatogonium (2n, mitosis) → primary spermatocyte (2n, DNA replicated, 4n DNA content) → Meiosis I → 2 secondary spermatocytes (n, haploid after meiosis I) → Meiosis II → 4 spermatids (n) → spermiogenesis → 4 spermatozoa (n). Sertoli cells supporting the process shown

options.B: Diploid (2n) – 2 sets of chromosomes but 4n DNA

question: The diagram shows oogenesis. How many functional eggs result from one primary oocyte?
diagram_description: Diagram of oogenesis: Primary oocyte (2n) undergoes Meiosis I (unequal cytokinesis) → Large secondary oocyte (n) + small first polar body. Secondary oocyte undergoes Meiosis II (only if fertilized) → Large ovum (n) + small second polar body. First polar body may also divide → 2 polar bodies. Total: 1 ovum + 3 polar bodies. Comparison with spermatogenesis (4 sperm) shown

question: The diagram shows a cross-section of the testis. Which cells are found in the interstitium between seminiferous tubules?
diagram_description: Cross-section of testis showing: Multiple seminiferous tubules (with developing sperm at different stages – spermatogonia at periphery, primary/secondary spermatocytes, spermatids, spermatozoa toward lumen), Sertoli cells (large, irregularly shaped, extending from basement membrane to lumen), Leydig cells (large, lipid droplet-rich cells in interstitial tissue BETWEEN tubules), blood vessels in interstitium

question: The diagram shows the female reproductive system. Identify the structure where fertilization typically occurs.
diagram_description: Diagram of female reproductive system showing: two ovaries (almond-shaped), fallopian tubes (uterine tubes) with fimbriae near ovaries and connecting to uterus at uterotubal junction, uterus (pear-shaped, thick muscular wall), cervix (lower narrow portion), vagina, broad ligament. Ampulla of fallopian tube labeled as site of fertilization. Endometrium and myometrium shown in uterine cross-section

question: The diagram shows the structure of the blastocyst. Which part forms the placenta?
diagram_description: Diagram of blastocyst (day 5–6): Outer layer = trophoblast cells (will form placenta and extraembryonic membranes, later differentiates into cytotrophoblast and syncytiotrophoblast), Inner cell mass (embryoblast, will form the embryo proper), Blastocoel (fluid-filled cavity), Zona pellucida (still present at day 5, shed before implantation at day 6)

question: The diagram shows different phases of the menstrual cycle. What structural change occurs in the endometrium during the secretory (luteal) phase?
diagram_description: Diagram of uterine (endometrial) cycle corresponding to ovarian cycle: Menstrual phase (days 1–5, endometrium shed), Proliferative/follicular phase (days 6–13, estrogen causes endometrium to thicken), Ovulation (day 14), Secretory/luteal phase (days 15–28, progesterone causes glands to secrete glycogen, uterus prepared for implantation). Cross-sections of endometrium at each phase shown

options.C: Endometrial glands secrete glycogen (uterus prepared for implantation)

question: The diagram shows a cross-section of a seminiferous tubule. Identify the cell that forms the blood-testis barrier.
diagram_description: Cross-section of seminiferous tubule showing: basement membrane at periphery, Sertoli cells (large columnar cells with irregular nuclei extending full width of tubule, tight junctions between adjacent Sertoli cells forming blood-testis barrier in middle region), spermatogonia (at periphery below barrier), primary and secondary spermatocytes and spermatids above barrier (adluminal compartment), spermatozoa in lumen

question: The diagram shows the stages of cleavage. What is the 8–16 cell stage called?
diagram_description: Diagram of early embryonic development: Zygote (1 cell, fertilized egg) → 2-cell stage (first cleavage) → 4-cell stage → 8-cell stage → Morula (16–32 solid ball of cells, 3–4 days) → Blastocyst (hollow ball with inner cell mass and fluid-filled blastocoel, day 5–6) → Implantation (day 6–7). Each stage labeled with timing

question: The diagram shows a sarcomere during contraction. What happens to the I-band and H-zone?
diagram_description: Three diagrams of sarcomere: (A) relaxed state – wide I-band (actin only), wide H-zone (myosin only), full A-band; (B) partially contracted – I-band narrowed, H-zone narrowed, A-band unchanged; (C) fully contracted – I-band almost disappeared, H-zone disappeared, A-band unchanged, Z-lines very close together

options.B: Both I-band and H-zone decrease; A-band unchanged

question: The diagram shows the neuromuscular junction. What is released from the motor nerve terminal to trigger muscle contraction?
diagram_description: Diagram of neuromuscular junction showing: motor neuron axon terminal (synaptic knob), synaptic vesicles containing acetylcholine, synaptic cleft, motor end plate (sarcolemma with acetylcholine receptors nicotinic type), muscle fibre below. Steps shown: action potential → Ca2+ entry → vesicle fusion → ACh release → ACh binds receptors → depolarization of sarcolemma → muscle action potential → contraction

question: The diagram shows the cross-bridge cycle in muscle contraction. At which step does ATP hydrolysis (ATPase) occur?
diagram_description: Cross-bridge cycle diagram: (1) Myosin head cocked (high energy) – ADP+Pi attached; (2) Cross-bridge attachment – myosin head binds to actin; (3) Power stroke – myosin head pivots, ADP+Pi released, filaments slide; (4) Rigor state – myosin remains attached; (5) ATP binds myosin – cross-bridge detaches; (6) ATP hydrolysis re-cocks myosin head. Arrows connecting steps

options.C: When ATP binds and myosin detaches, re-cocking the head

question: The diagram shows different types of joints. Identify the synovial joint type found at the shoulder.
diagram_description: Diagrams of joint types: (A) Fibrous (suture) – no cavity, bones connected by fibrous tissue (skull); (B) Cartilaginous – slight movement, fibrocartilage disc between bones (vertebrae); (C) Hinge – uniaxial movement (elbow, knee); (D) Ball and socket – multiaxial, spherical head in cup-shaped socket (shoulder – humerus in glenoid cavity, hip – femur in acetabulum)

question: The diagram shows a cross-section of skeletal muscle with different fibre types stained. Which fibre type (Type I) is fatigue-resistant?
diagram_description: Cross-section of skeletal muscle histochemical stain showing: Type I fibres (slow oxidative – dark stain, small diameter, many mitochondria, high myoglobin – red, used for endurance); Type IIa fibres (fast oxidative glycolytic – intermediate stain, medium diameter); Type IIb/IIx fibres (fast glycolytic – light stain, large diameter, few mitochondria, fatigue quickly – used for power). Labelled with properties

question: The diagram shows the action of calcium in muscle contraction. Where does Ca2+ bind to initiate contraction?
diagram_description: Diagram showing thin filament structure: actin monomers forming double helix, tropomyosin strands in grooves blocking myosin-binding sites, troponin complex (TnI, TnC, TnT) at regular intervals. In resting state: tropomyosin blocks sites. When Ca2+ released from SR, Ca2+ binds to TnC → conformational change → tropomyosin shifts → myosin binding sites exposed on actin

question: The diagram shows bone remodeling. Which cells break down bone tissue (resorption)?
diagram_description: Diagram of bone remodeling cycle: Osteoclasts (large multinucleated cells, derived from monocytes) on bone surface creating resorption pit (Howship's lacunae), dissolving mineral by HCl and enzymes. Osteoblasts (single nucleus, derived from mesenchymal stem cells) depositing new osteoid (collagen + mineral). Osteocytes (mature osteoblasts trapped in lacunae) maintaining bone. RANKL/OPG signaling pathway shown

question: The diagram shows different types of fractures. Which fracture type is common in osteoporotic patients?
diagram_description: Diagrams of fracture types: (A) Transverse – straight break across bone shaft; (B) Spiral – bone twisted, fracture spirals around shaft; (C) Comminuted – bone shattered into multiple fragments (common in elderly/osteoporotic bones due to brittle, low-density bone); (D) Greenstick – incomplete fracture, bone bends (children)

question: The diagram shows an action potential. What happens at threshold potential?
diagram_description: Graph of action potential showing membrane potential (mV) vs time (ms): resting potential at -70mV, threshold at -55mV (where Na+ channels open), depolarization phase rising to +40mV, repolarization (K+ channels open), after-hyperpolarization below -70mV (refractory period), return to resting. Each phase labeled with ionic events

options.B: Voltage-gated Na+ channels open causing rapid depolarization
options.D: K+ ions rush into the cell

question: The diagram shows the structure of the human eye. Which structure focuses light onto the retina?
diagram_description: Sagittal section of human eye showing: cornea (transparent outer layer, does most refraction), aqueous humor, iris with pupil, lens (biconvex, adjustable focus/accommodation), vitreous humor, retina (photoreceptor layer), macula with fovea (highest acuity), optic disc/blind spot, optic nerve, choroid (vascular layer), sclera (white outer coat)

question: The diagram shows the reflex arc. Which component receives and processes the signal to produce a response?
diagram_description: Diagram of reflex arc showing: receptor (skin/muscle spindle) → afferent (sensory) neuron → nerve centre in spinal cord (interneuron/integration center, shown as synapse in grey matter) → efferent (motor) neuron → effector (muscle/gland). Spinal cord cross-section shown with grey matter (butterfly shape) and white matter around it

question: The diagram shows the cochlea. Which part contains the organ of Corti?
diagram_description: Cross-section of cochlea (unrolled) showing three fluid-filled canals: Scala vestibuli (above basilar membrane, contains perilymph), Scala media/cochlear duct (between, contains endolymph, bounded by Reissner's membrane above and basilar membrane below), Scala tympani (below basilar membrane, contains perilymph). Organ of Corti labeled on basilar membrane in scala media, with hair cells, tectorial membrane above

options.C: Scala media (cochlear duct) on basilar membrane

question: The diagram shows synaptic transmission. What happens when EPSP (excitatory post-synaptic potential) summation reaches threshold?
diagram_description: Diagram of synapse showing presynaptic terminal with vesicles, synaptic cleft, post-synaptic membrane with receptors. Graph showing: subthreshold EPSP1 (small depolarization), subthreshold EPSP2, temporal summation (EPSP1+EPSP2 reaching threshold → action potential). Spatial summation also shown. Threshold line marked. IPSP (hyperpolarization) also shown for inhibitory synapse

options.A: An action potential is generated in the post-synaptic neuron

question: The diagram shows the retina layers. Which cells convert light to electrical signals (phototransduction)?
diagram_description: Cross-section of retina showing layers from outside (choroid) inward: (1) Pigment epithelium; (2) Photoreceptors – rods (dim light, peripheral) and cones (color, fovea); (3) Bipolar cells; (4) Ganglion cells; (5) Optic nerve fibres → optic disc. Light enters from ganglion cell side and reaches photoreceptors at back

question: The diagram shows the distribution of hearing along the basilar membrane. Where are high-frequency sounds detected?
diagram_description: Diagram of unrolled cochlea showing basilar membrane with tonotopic organization: base of cochlea (near oval window) – narrow, stiff basilar membrane detects HIGH frequencies (20,000 Hz); apex of cochlea (helicotrema) – wide, flexible basilar membrane detects LOW frequencies (20 Hz). Frequency map shown along membrane with values

options.C: Base of cochlea near oval window

question: The diagram shows the autonomic nervous system innervation of the heart. Which division slows heart rate?
diagram_description: Diagram showing autonomic innervation: Sympathetic nervous system (thoracolumbar outflow T1–L2) – preganglionic neurons → sympathetic ganglia → postganglionic noradrenergic fibres → heart (increases HR, force). Parasympathetic nervous system (craniosacral outflow, vagus nerve CN X) – preganglionic fibres to intramural ganglia → short postganglionic cholinergic fibres → SA node (decreases HR, force)

question: The diagram shows the visual pathway. What is the visual field representation in the right occipital cortex?
diagram_description: Diagram of visual pathway: Both eyes → optic nerves → optic chiasma (nasal fibres cross) → optic tract → lateral geniculate nucleus (thalamus) → optic radiation → primary visual cortex (striate cortex, V1) in occipital lobe. Right occipital cortex receives: temporal fibres of right eye (right visual field, right eye) + nasal fibres of left eye (also right visual field, left eye) = LEFT visual field of both eyes

options.A: Right visual field of both eyes
options.B: Left visual field of both eyes

question: The diagram shows predator-prey population cycles. What happens to prey population when predator numbers increase?
diagram_description: Graph showing cyclic oscillations of predator (foxes) and prey (rabbits) populations over time. Prey numbers rise → predator numbers follow and rise (lag) → prey numbers fall as predation increases → predator numbers fall (less food) → prey numbers rise again. Classic Lotka-Volterra cycle shown. Prey curve leads predator curve by approximately one quarter cycle

question: The diagram shows J-curve vs S-curve population growth. What does the S-shaped (sigmoid) curve represent?
diagram_description: Two growth curves: J-shaped curve (exponential growth, dN/dt = rN) – population grows without limit. S-shaped/sigmoid curve (logistic growth, dN/dt = rN[(K-N)/K]) – slow initial growth (lag phase), rapid growth (log phase), slowing as K approached, plateau at K (carrying capacity). K line shown as horizontal asymptote. Inflection point at K/2 where growth rate is maximum

options.B: Logistic growth with carrying capacity (K) as upper limit

question: The diagram shows three types of age pyramids. Which pyramid represents a stable population?
diagram_description: Three age pyramids (pre-reproductive, reproductive, post-reproductive age groups shown as horizontal bars): (A) Expanding – very broad base (many young), narrow top, triangular shape; (B) Stable – roughly equal width pre-reproductive and reproductive ages, bell/column shape; (C) Declining – narrow base (few young), wider middle, inverted triangle. Countries labeled as examples

options.B: Pyramid B (stable – roughly equal age classes)

question: The diagram shows the concept of ecological niche. The fundamental niche vs realized niche differs because:
diagram_description: Two-dimensional niche diagram with axes Temperature and Food size. Fundamental niche shown as larger ellipse (full range of conditions species could occupy without competition). Realized niche shown as smaller ellipse within fundamental (actual conditions occupied when competitors present). Competitive exclusion narrows realized niche. Niche overlap between two species shown leading to competition

options.B: Realized niche is smaller than fundamental niche due to interspecific competition
options.D: Fundamental niche depends on climate only

question: The diagram shows Lotka-Volterra predation equations. At what prey density is predator growth rate zero?
diagram_description: Lotka-Volterra equations: Prey: dH/dt = rH – aHP (r = prey growth, a = predation rate, P = predator). Predator: dP/dt = baHP – mP (b = conversion efficiency, m = predator mortality). Zero isoclines plotted: prey isocline (vertical line at H = m/ab) – predator population stable; predator isocline (horizontal line at P = r/a) – prey population stable. Where isoclines cross = equilibrium. Populations cycle around equilibrium

options.B: When prey density equals m/(ab) – the predator's zero isocline

question: The diagram shows age-specific survivorship curves. Which type (Type I) represents large mammals?
diagram_description: Three survivorship curves plotted as log(survivors) vs age (percentage of lifespan): Type I (convex curve) – most individuals survive to old age then die rapidly; characteristic of large mammals (humans, elephants) with low early mortality and high parental care. Type II (diagonal line) – constant mortality at all ages (some birds, reptiles). Type III (concave curve) – high early mortality, survivors live long (fish, oysters, most invertebrates, plants)

options.B: Type I (convex, low early mortality – large mammals)

question: The diagram shows timing of amniocentesis. When is it usually performed?
diagram_description: Diagram of pregnant uterus at 15-20 weeks showing ultrasound-guided needle inserted through maternal abdominal wall into amniotic fluid avoiding fetus. Amniotic fluid with fetal cells withdrawn. Timeline showing amniocentesis at 15-20 weeks vs CVS at 10-12 weeks. Fetal cells cultured for karyotyping and AFP measured for neural tube defects.

question: The diagram shows how HIV attacks immune cells. Which cell is the primary target?
diagram_description: Diagram showing HIV gp120 binding to CD4 receptor and CCR5 co-receptor on CD4+ T helper lymphocyte. Virus fuses, inserts RNA, reverse transcriptase converts RNA to DNA, integrates into host genome. Graph shows CD4 count decreasing over years while viral load rises. AIDS defined when CD4 < 200/uL.

question: The diagram shows sperm analysis for male infertility. What is normal sperm count per mL?
diagram_description: Semen analysis report showing normal parameters: Volume >1.5 mL, Sperm concentration >15 million/mL (WHO criteria), Total motility >40%, Progressive motility >32%, Normal morphology >4% Kruger strict. Below-normal values shown for oligospermia, asthenospermia and teratospermia with example values.

question: The diagram shows the ELISA test used to detect HIV antibodies. A positive result means:
diagram_description: ELISA diagram: HIV antigens coated on well, patient serum added (anti-HIV antibodies bind if present), secondary enzyme-linked antibody added, substrate added producing color change in positive wells. Western blot shown as confirmatory test. Sensitivity ~99.7% and specificity ~99.9% labeled.

options.B: HIV antibodies present (person infected, requires Western blot confirmation)

question: The diagram shows structure of HIV. Which protein binds to CD4 cells?
diagram_description: Diagram of HIV virion showing lipid envelope derived from host, envelope glycoproteins gp120 (outer knob binding CD4) and gp41 (transmembrane), two copies of RNA genome, reverse transcriptase RT, integrase, protease, p24 capsid protein, matrix protein p17. Each component labeled with function.

question: The diagram shows different types of epithelial tissues. Identify the pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium.
diagram_description: Four diagrams of epithelial tissue cross-sections: (A) single layer of flat cells; (B) single layer of tall column-shaped cells with cilia at apical surface, nuclei at different heights giving false appearance of stratification; (C) multiple distinct layers of cells; (D) single layer of cube-shaped cells

question: Study the diagram of a sarcomere showing different bands. What is the region between two Z-lines called?
diagram_description: Diagram of sarcomere showing: Z-line, I-band (actin only), A-band (actin+myosin overlap), H-zone (myosin only), M-line in center, with labels indicating thin (actin) and thick (myosin) filaments

question: The diagram shows the internal anatomy of a frog. Identify the organ that serves BOTH digestive and excretory functions.
diagram_description: Ventral dissection diagram of frog showing: oral cavity, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver (large three-lobed), gall bladder, pancreas, kidneys (dorsal), fat bodies, gonads, urinary bladder, cloaca

question: The diagram shows the cross-section of bone tissue. Identify the structure that connects adjacent Haversian systems.
diagram_description: Cross-section of compact bone showing: Haversian canal at center, concentric lamellae around it, osteocytes in lacunae, canaliculi connecting lacunae, Volkmann's canals running perpendicular to Haversian canals, periosteum on outside

question: The diagram shows types of cartilage. In which location is fibrocartilage found?
diagram_description: Three diagrams of cartilage types showing matrix composition: (A) Hyaline – clear glassy matrix with scattered chondrocytes; (B) Elastic – matrix with elastic fibres; (C) Fibrocartilage – matrix with dense collagen fibres, chondrocytes in rows between fibre bundles. Locations for each type indicated

question: The diagram of cockroach's nervous system shows nerve ganglia. How many ganglia are present in the abdominal region?
diagram_description: Ventral nervous system of cockroach showing: supra-oesophageal ganglion (brain) in head, sub-oesophageal ganglion, 3 thoracic ganglia in thoracic segments, 6 abdominal ganglia in abdomen, all connected by double ventral nerve cord

question: The diagram shows a neuron. Which part is responsible for receiving signals from other neurons?
diagram_description: Diagram of multipolar neuron showing: cell body (soma) with nucleus, multiple branching dendrites extending from soma, single long axon with myelin sheath, nodes of Ranvier, axon terminals (synaptic knobs), arrows indicating direction of impulse

question: The diagram shows the cross-section of skeletal muscle fibre. What is the functional unit of contraction?
diagram_description: Cross-section and longitudinal section of skeletal muscle showing: muscle fibre with multiple peripheral nuclei, myofibrils running longitudinally, sarcomere between Z-lines, actin (thin filaments) and myosin (thick filaments), sarcoplasmic reticulum surrounding myofibrils, T-tubules at A-I junction
